macro_segments.title: Hearing requirement for Shema and other mitzvot: initial mishna interpretation and the debate between Rabbi Yose and Rabbi Yehuda

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the mishna debate: Rabbi Yose vs. Rabbi Yehuda on hearing what you say
Gemara's question: determining which Tanna holds which position regarding deaf persons reading Megillah
Gemara's attempt to attribute the Megillah mishna to Rabbi Yehuda with conditional permissions
Proving Rabbi Yehuda allows hearing requirement only b'di'avad through Terumah source

macro_segments.title: Resolving conflicting positions on whether hearing is required l'chatchila: Rabbi Yehuda vs. his Rebbe Rabbi Elazar ben Azarya and Rabbi Meir

micro_segments.title: Gemara introduces another braita showing only b'di'avad requirement: braita of not blessing in one's heart
Two possible solutions: either Rabbi Yehuda or his Rebbe differ, or Rabbi Yehuda differs from Rabbi Meir
Gemara's final resolution: Rabbi Yehuda doesn't require hearing even l'chatchila; his Rebbe Rabbi Elazar ben Azarya requires it l'chatchila

macro_segments.title: Halachic ruling and scope limitation: Rav Chisda rules l'chatchila hearing required, b'di'avad sufficient; debate applies only to Shema

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's ruling in name of Rav Shila: require hearing l'chatchila but permit b'di'avad
Rav Yosef limits the debate to Shema only; other mitzvot require hearing or you don't fulfill obligation
Gemara challenges with braita of blessing Birkat Hamazon; resolution that dispute is uniquely about Shema

macro_segments.title: Precise articulation of Shema and distinctions in letters: reading precisely and giving spaces between words

micro_segments.title: Mishna rule: reading Shema without precision in letters is still valid after the fact
Rabbi Tevi in name of Rabbi Yoshiya: we rule leniently both for not hearing and imprecise letters

macro_segments.title: Digression on resurrection through comparative hermeneutics: Sheol and womb as kal vachomer argument

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Tevi on Rabbi Yoshiya: interpretation of 'three things never sated' comparing womb and Sheol
Kal vachomer argument: if womb produces publicly despite private conception, Sheol will resurrect publicly

macro_segments.title: Writing and teaching requirements: derashos from vetchavtem and v'limadetem for mezuza completeness

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Oshia teaches Rava: entire parsha including vetchavtem command must be written in mezuza
Rava's response: explains why derashah needed despite Sotah precedent; vetchavtem is more general

macro_segments.title: Writing and teaching requirements: derashos from vetchavtem and v'limadetem for mezuza completeness (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ovadia teaches Rava: v'limadetem teaches completeness in learning and giving space
Rava interprets as practical requirement: don't merge words ending with same letter as next word starts

macro_segments.title: Precise articulation of Shema and distinctions in letters: reading precisely and giving spaces between words (continued)

micro_segments.title: Derashah from 'v'limadetem': give space between words that would run together; examples from Shema text
Encouragement: one who reads Shema carefully with proper letters receives reward and Gehennom is cooled
Tosafos expands on Rif's additions: careful distinction of similar sounds and ending/beginning letters

macro_segments.title: Reading out of order and mistakes in Shema: halacha for recovering from errors and Rabbi Yochanan's principle

micro_segments.title: Mishna rule: reading out of order means you don't fulfill obligation
Story of Rabbi Elazar: visiting Beis Medrash to learn while chuppah is being made
Tanna's teaching to Rabbi Yochanan: procedures for going back when mistake location is unknown
Rabbi Yochanan's principle: if you reached l'maan yirbu yomeichem, assume correct from there onward
Rabbi Elazar reports teaching; Rabbi Ami and Assi affirm the principle's value

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yose vs. Rabbi Yehuda debate
